lesson.menu_desc: Coping when plans go wrong & talking about hypotheticals -- 35 slides
lesson.subtitle: Aula 15 -- Handling the Unexpected
lesson.title_tag: Professor View -- Pricila Adamo | Aula 15 -- Handling the Unexpected

paras: Halfway through what was meant to be a relaxing trip, everything went pear-shaped at once. My connecting flight was cancelled, the airline had no record of my onward booking, and the only hotel I had reserved for that night had quietly given my room away. Standing in a foreign airport at midnight, jet-lagged and exhausted, I felt the panic rising. For a few minutes I could not think on my feet at all; I just stared at the departure board as if it might fix itself.

Then something my mother used to say came back to me: when a trip goes wrong, the journey is not ruined -- it just changes shape. So I made do with what I had. I took it in my stride, kept my cool, and worked the problem one piece at a time. I found a quiet corner, charged my phone, and built a quick contingency plan: a new route home, a cheaper hotel two streets away, and a backup plan in case that one fell through too. By the time the sun came up, I had weathered the storm, and the curveball had somehow become the best story of the whole trip.

Looking back, the trip taught me more about myself than any smooth holiday ever could. If it happened again tomorrow, I would not panic the way I did at first. I would breathe, accept the hiccup, and trust that I can roll with the punches. The unexpected is not the enemy of a good journey; very often, it is the part you remember most.

gist.prompt: What is the main idea of the text?
choices: Careful planning makes sure nothing ever goes wrong on a trip abroad.
When things go wrong while travelling, staying calm and improvising can turn a crisis into the trip's best memory.
The writer decided never to travel alone again after such a stressful night.

items: The writer's connecting flight, onward booking and hotel room all failed on the same night.
t
The flight was cancelled, the airline had no record of the booking, and the hotel had given the room away.
At first, the writer was able to think on their feet and solve everything calmly.
f
For a few minutes the writer could not think on their feet at all and just stared at the board.
The writer built a contingency plan with a new route, a cheaper hotel and a backup option.
t
They planned a new route home, a cheaper hotel two streets away, and a backup plan in case that fell through.
Looking back, the writer regrets the trip and wishes it had been smooth.
f
The writer says the trip taught them more about themselves than any smooth holiday could.
Scenario 1
Your flight home is cancelled at 11 p.m. and the airline desk has just closed. What would you do?
Scenario 2
You arrive at your hotel after a long journey and they say they have no record of your booking. How would you handle it?
Scenario 3
On the morning of a big day trip, it starts pouring with rain and your outdoor plan is ruined. What would you do instead?
Scenario 4
You lose your bag with your charger, medication and half your clothes in it. How would you cope for the next two days?

lf.title: How calm vs anxious coping sounds
items: Calm
Don't worry,
we'll just roll with the punches
and sort it out.
soft
Anxious
This is a disaster -- everything is ruined!
strong
Calm
It's only a hiccup;
let's keep our cool
and find another way.
soft
Anxious
I have no idea what to do, I can't cope with this!
strong
Calm
We have a backup plan, so
if this falls through
, we still have options.
soft
Real: If I miss the train, I will take a taxi. Make it hypothetical.
if I missed
Real: If the hotel loses my booking, I will ask for the manager. Make it hypothetical.
if the hotel lost
Real: If it rains, we will change our plans. Make it hypothetical.
if it rained
Real: If I lose my passport, I will go to the embassy. Make it hypothetical.
if I lost
Advice: You are in my situation. Give advice with a hypothetical.
if I were you

answerkey.title: Reveal the rephrase answer key
list: If I missed the train, I would take a taxi.
If the hotel lost my booking, I would ask for the manager.
If it rained, we would change our plans.
If I lost my passport, I would go to the embassy.
If I were you, I would stay calm and make a backup plan.
answerkey.note: Second conditional form: if + PAST SIMPLE, would + base verb. The past tense here is hypothetical, not real past. Never put would in the if-clause (NOT 'if I would lose'). Use were for all persons in the hypothetical ('if I were you', 'if the flight were cancelled').

analyse.title: Close reading
items: Find the exact moment the writer stops panicking and starts coping. What sentence marks the turning point, and what triggers it?
The writer lists three parts of their contingency plan. What are they, and why does having a backup plan matter so much here?
In the last paragraph the writer says what they would do differently if it happened again. How does the second conditional let them reflect without regret?

bank.label: Useful language for handling the unexpected
items: Let's not panic -- we can roll with the punches.
It's just a hiccup; we'll sort it out.
What's our backup plan if this falls through?
If I were you, I'd stay calm and think it through.
We'll make do with what we have for tonight.
Let's take it in our stride and work the problem.
What would you do if the flight were cancelled?
We've weathered worse than this before.

listenings.text: I want to tell you about the night a perfect trip completely fell through. I was changing planes in a city I had never been to, and within an hour, everything went pear-shaped. My connection was cancelled, the airline had lost my onward booking, and my hotel had given my room to someone else. For a few minutes I just froze; I honestly could not think on my feet at all. But then I made a choice. I decided to keep my cool and treat it as one big puzzle, not one big disaster. I made do with a quiet corner and a phone charger, and I built a quick contingency plan: a new route home, a cheaper hotel nearby, and a backup plan in case that one fell through too. By sunrise I had weathered the storm. And here is the strange part: that curveball became the best story of the whole trip. If it happened again, I honestly would not panic. I would take it in my stride, because now I know I can roll with the punches.

Hi Pricila, it's me, leaving you a quick voice note before your big trip next month. I just want to give you one piece of advice, traveller to traveller. Something will probably go wrong at some point. A flight gets delayed, a booking disappears, the weather turns. It always does, and that's fine. If I were you, I wouldn't see it as a disaster. I would treat every hiccup as part of the adventure. The travellers who enjoy themselves the most are not the ones with perfect plans; they're the ones who keep their cool and roll with the punches. So before you leave, think about your worst-case scenario for a minute, make a simple backup plan, and then forget about it. If something falls through while you're away, you'll think on your feet and handle it. And if you ever feel caught off guard, just breathe, take it in your stride, and remember: the best stories almost always come from the moments that did not go as planned. Have an amazing trip.

extra_audio.text: First, their hotel booking fell through at the very last minute. Although it was a real curveball, Pricila kept her cool. Thinking on her feet, she used her backup plan. They had to make do with a smaller room for one night. In the end, she took the whole thing in her stride and weathered the storm.
